sourceTitle: This notes & docs system makes your design team more efficient

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of having a centralized Meeting Notes and Docs system for a design team?

options.text: It reduces the amount of design work required for each project.
options.explanation: Centralizing notes doesn’t change the amount of design work; it improves information flow.

options.text: It increases transparency and helps everyone stay aligned even if they missed a meeting.
options.explanation: A unified system lets anyone access meeting context and documentation, fostering transparency and alignment.

options.text: It automatically generates design assets for the team.
options.explanation: Notion stores information but does not create design assets on its own.

options.text: It eliminates the need for regular stand‑up meetings.
options.explanation: The system records stand‑up notes; it doesn’t replace the meetings themselves.

questions.question: Which Notion database view is recommended for creating a minimalist collection of pages for Meeting Notes and Docs?

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view is useful for Kanban style workflows, but the guide suggests a List view for simplicity.

options.text: Table view
options.explanation: Table view shows rows and columns; the guide specifically mentions using a List.

options.text: List – Full page view
options.explanation: The article instructs to type "/list" and choose "List – Full page" for a clean, minimalist collection.

options.text: Calendar view
options.explanation: Calendar view organizes items by date, which isn’t the primary recommendation for meeting notes.

questions.question: Which property type should you use to tag participants in a meeting note?

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select creates custom tags, but it’s not ideal for assigning people.

options.text: Person
options.explanation: The Person property lets you tag Notion users, making it perfect for meeting participants.

options.text: Date
options.explanation: Date records a point in time, not a person.

options.text: Number
options.explanation: Number stores numeric values, not user references.

questions.question: How can sorting and filtering improve efficiency when using the Docs list?

options.text: They automatically rewrite the content of each document to match a style guide.
options.explanation: Sorting and filtering only affect how items are displayed; they don’t edit content.

options.text: They let you see only the most relevant documents—such as the newest drafts or specific types—without scanning the entire list.
options.explanation: By applying filters (e.g., by Type or Status) and sorts (e.g., Last edited descending), you surface the items you need instantly.

options.text: They merge duplicate documents into a single entry.
options.explanation: Merging duplicates is a manual action; sorting/filtering only changes view order.

options.text: They lock the database so no one can add new pages.
options.explanation: Sorting/filtering does not affect permissions.

questions.question: What is the advantage of creating multiple permanent views in a Notion database?

options.text: Each view automatically syncs with an external spreadsheet.
options.explanation: Views are internal to Notion; they don’t auto‑export to spreadsheets.

options.text: Team members can instantly switch between predefined filters, sorts, or layouts without rebuilding them each time.
options.explanation: Permanent views store specific configurations (e.g., a board filtered by Status) so you can toggle between them instantly.

options.text: They prevent other users from editing the underlying data.
options.explanation: Views are just different ways to see data; they don’t change edit permissions.

options.text: They duplicate the database so each view has its own separate set of pages.
options.explanation: All views reference the same database; they don’t create copies.

questions.question: When customizing a page for a design spec, which header hierarchy is recommended for organizing sections like "Problem statement", "Goals", and "Screenshot examples"?

options.text: Use H3 for the main sections and H4 for sub‑points.
options.explanation: H3 is too low‑level for top‑level sections; the guide suggests H1/H2 for primary headings.

options.text: Use H1 for the document title, then H2 for each major section (Problem statement, Goals, Screenshot examples).
options.explanation: The article advises using H1, H2, H3 hierarchy, with H2 for main sections under the page title.

options.text: Use plain text only; headers are not necessary.
options.explanation: Headers improve readability; the guide recommends them.

options.text: Use bullet points instead of headers.
options.explanation: Bullets list items but don’t provide the structural hierarchy that headers give.

questions.question: Which embed types are specifically mentioned as useful for designers to include in their docs?

options.text: Google Docs and Dropbox files
options.explanation: Those services are not listed in the article; the focus is on design‑specific tools.

options.text: Figma, InVision, Framer, Whimsical, Miro, and Abstract projects
options.explanation: The guide explicitly lists these design‑focused embed options.

options.text: Spotify playlists and SoundCloud tracks
options.explanation: Audio embeds are not mentioned as design‑relevant in the article.

options.text: Twitter timelines and Instagram feeds
options.explanation: Social media embeds are not part of the recommended design toolset.

questions.question: How does using a template for recurring meetings (e.g., weekly standups) benefit the team?

options.text: It guarantees that every meeting will be exactly one hour long.
options.explanation: Templates provide structure, not time limits.

options.text: It ensures consistent headings, properties, and checklists across all instances, saving time and reducing missing information.
options.explanation: Templates pre‑populate headers, tags, and sections, so each meeting note starts with the same format.

options.text: It automatically records a video of the meeting.
options.explanation: Templates are static page structures; they don’t record video.

options.text: It locks the page so participants cannot edit the agenda.
options.explanation: Templates are meant to be duplicated and then edited; they don’t enforce read‑only status.

questions.question: Which scenario best illustrates moving a page from the Docs list to a team wiki, and why is this action useful?

options.text: A finished RFC for a new brand font is moved to the wiki so the entire company can reference the final design guidelines, keeping the active Docs list focused on work‑in‑progress items.
options.explanation: Moving completed, reference‑ready documents out of the active list declutters the workspace while preserving easy access via the wiki.

options.text: An early‑stage brainstorming note is moved to the wiki to hide it from the rest of the team.
options.explanation: Early drafts should stay in the Docs list for collaboration; moving them to the wiki would reduce visibility.

options.text: A meeting note from a stand‑up is moved to the wiki to replace the original stand‑up page.
options.explanation: Stand‑up notes remain useful in the Meeting Notes list; moving them would break the chronological record.

options.text: A design spec that is still awaiting feedback is moved to the wiki to signal that it is final.
options.explanation: If feedback is still needed, the spec should stay in the active Docs list, not be treated as final.

questions.question: If you want to view only design documents that are "Open for Comments" and were authored by a specific designer, which combination of Notion features must you configure?

options.text: Create a new database, add a Formula property that concatenates status and author, then filter on that formula.
options.explanation: A Formula can combine fields, but the simpler approach is to filter on the existing Status (Select) and Author (Person) properties.

options.text: Add a Select property for Status, a Person property for Author, then set a Filter that includes Status = "Open for Comments" AND Author = the chosen designer.
options.explanation: Filtering on both the Status Select and the Person (Author) property directly yields the desired view.

options.text: Use a Calendar view and sort by date, then manually scroll to find the right pages.
options.explanation: Calendar view and manual scrolling do not guarantee the specific combination of status and author.

options.text: Apply a Sort on the Priority property and rely on the default view to show the correct items.
options.explanation: Sorting does not filter; it only changes order, so it won’t limit results to the desired status and author.
